text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
In our previous studies we looked closely at the Ark of the Covenant; what it contained, what every part of it pointed to in Christ, and what it means that the congregation carries it on their shoulders today. Now we move forward to the second item on the Kohathites’ list. The next thing they lifted onto their shoulders and carried through the wilderness was the Table of Showbread.
This was not a plain piece of furniture pushed into a corner. Every part of it was deliberate, every material was chosen by God, and every detail was speaking. Today we begin to look at this table; its names, its construction, and where it stood.

The Table of Showbread had three different names in Scripture, and each name opens a different door into what this table truly was. The first name was Lechem Panim; the Bread of the Faces, or the Bread of the Presence. The word panim means faces. This bread stood before the face of Yahweh. Not behind Him, not beside Him, not occasionally brought near; always before His face, always in His direct sight, always present before the one who sees everything. The second name was Lechem Tamid, the Continual Bread. Tamid is a Hebrew word meaning without ceasing, perpetually, always. This bread was never absent. From the day the tabernacle was set up until the day it was taken down, there was never a morning when the table stood empty. It was there every day, every week, every year through forty years of wilderness wandering.

The third name was Shulkhan, simply meaning table. But this word carries more than we might first suppose. The Hebrew word shulkhan comes from the verb shalakh, meaning to send or to spread out. A table is something that is spread out for a purpose. It is not a storage unit. It is not a shelf. It is a surface prepared for fellowship and provision, a place where someone is welcomed in and given something to eat. This is the very word used in Psalm 23:5, where David sings to Yahweh: “You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies.” The same word. The same God. God was not simply storing bread inside the tabernacle. He was spreading a table; declaring with the furniture of His house that there is fellowship here, there is provision here, and that a King receives His people before His face.

The instructions for the table are given in Exodus 25:23-30. The table was made of acacia wood, the same shittim wood used for the Ark. This wood has a hard, dense, reddish-brown heartwood and is well known for its resistance to decay even under the most difficult desert conditions. God chose wood that endures. Over this wood, beaten sheets of pure gold were laid as a covering, clothing the entire table. The combination is the same as the Ark, acacia wood inside, pure gold outside. The earthly clothed in the heavenly. The corruptible covered by the incorruptible. There is a message here that we will open tomorrow when we look at what the table points to in Christ. But for now, simply notice the materials; God chose the same two materials for His table as for the throne of His presence.

Around the top edge of the table ran a gold border, described in Scripture as a crown. A handbreadth-wide second border with gold molding ran lower around the sides. These rims served a practical purpose, keeping the vessels and bread from sliding off during the journey. But they were also a declaration. The table wore a crown. It belonged to a King. Even the edges of this table were dressed in royalty. Below these borders, at the four corners where the legs met the tabletop, four gold rings were fixed. Through these rings, acacia wood poles overlaid with gold were inserted so the table could be carried. It was never dragged. It was never loaded onto a cart. It was lifted on the shoulders of the Kohathites, carried through the sand and the sun and the long wilderness miles, step by step, for forty years.

The table was placed along the north wall of the Holy Place, just outside the veil that separated the Most Holy Place. As the priests entered, the table stood on the right; always before the face of Yahweh. And here is something worth pausing over. The table was the same height as the Ark of the Covenant.
These two pieces of furniture; the throne of God’s presence and the table of God’s provision, stood at exactly the same level. The presence and the bread were never far apart. God did not place His provision beneath His presence, as if bread were a lesser concern than glory. He placed them at the same height. This is the kind of God He is; the one whose presence and whose provision are inseparable.

The table was never bare. Four types of vessels sat on it at all times, and every single vessel was made of pure gold. Not one vessel was made from silver, bronze, or ordinary metal. Everything on God’s table was gold. The first vessels were the flat dishes or plates on which the loaves rested. The bread was never placed directly on the table’s surface. It was always placed on vessels, always elevated, always honoured. Even the bread’s resting place was treated with the highest care. The second were small golden cups filled with pure frankincense; one cup placed on top of each pile of six loaves. This frankincense was not burned on the table. When the bread was replaced each Sabbath, the frankincense was taken away and burned separately as a memorial fire offering before Yahweh. The bread represented the people standing before God. The burning incense represented their prayers and worship rising before His face.

The third type of vessel was the larger bowls, and the fourth were the pitchers used for pouring. Both were connected to the drink offerings poured alongside the bread. Where there was bread on God’s table, there was also the cup.
Table fellowship in God’s presence always involved both. Every single vessel, every dish, every cup, every bowl, every pitcher; was made of pure gold.
When God spreads a table, He uses nothing inferior. This table was not dressed down for the wilderness. It was dressed in the best; because the God it belonged to never reduces the honour of His own house simply because the surroundings are hard.

Now a question to carry into tomorrow. We have seen the table; its names, its wood, its gold, its crown, its vessels, its position before the face of Yahweh. But the table itself was only the beginning. What sat on top of this gold table? What was the bread, how was it made, who baked it, how was it arranged, and what happened to it every Sabbath? And when we have seen all of that; what does any of it have to do with us? What are we carrying on our shoulders when we carry this table through the wilderness of this age? We will continue tomorrow.

title: Prayer
text:
How lovely is Your dwelling place, Yahweh of hosts; our soul longs and faints for Your courts (Psalm 84:1-2). You are the God who spreads a table even in the wilderness. You did not wait until Israel arrived in a comfortable place before You set out Your provision. You spread the table in the desert, in the heat, in the dust, in the middle of the long journey. We thank You that Your table is not seasonal; that Your provision does not depend on our circumstances.
Forgive us for the times we have looked at our wilderness and concluded that the table must be empty. Help us to see what was always there; the bread before Your face, the cups of gold, the crown on the rim, the presence and the provision standing at the same height. Open our hearts to receive what You have prepared. And help us carry this table faithfully through every hard stretch of the journey. In Christ’s name, amen
